data.title: Tenaya Therapeutics: Risky But Promising Cardiac Gene Platform
data.description:
Tenaya Therapeutics, Inc. (TNYA) is a genetic medicine company focused on cardiac conditions. It leverages three interconnected systems: AAV9-based gene therapy, next-generation genetic modulation, and genetically informed small molecules. The company aims to turn its platform into a replicable engine for treating several inherited cardiomyopathies and related heart diseases. TNYA's pipeline includes gene therapies for one-time treatments in mutation-defined populations. One is TN-201 for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and the other is TN-401 for ARVC. Lastly, they have a third program called TN-301, which is a small molecule that targets a larger cardiometabolic population. However, I think their tight runway and regulatory setbacks warrant a neutral "Hold" for now.

Cardiomyopathic Treatments

Tenaya Therapeutics, Inc. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company specializing in developing therapies for inherited cardiomyopathies. The company was founded back in 2016 and is currently headquartered in South San Francisco, California.

As a quick overview, TNYA uses three systems that feed into each other. These are 1) gene therapy, 2) genetic modulation, and 3) precision small molecules. TNYA's gene therapy uses AAV9 to fix or replace faulty genes. AAV9 is a specific type of adeno-associated virus that is effective for reaching heart muscle cells called cardiomyocytes. This way, TNYA can package a healthy copy of a gene inside this viral shell and deliver it as a one-time IV infusion.

Moreover, TNYA has internal process development and manufacturing capacity for its gene therapies. Interestingly, manufacturing AAV therapies is a replicable process, and by extension, it's theoretically adaptable for other treatments. TNYA's system is a reusable engine that potentially can produce many types of heart-focused genetic medicines. TNYA argues it has built a modality-agnostic cardiac genetic-medicine platform with a large library of heart-tropic AAVs. This includes over 50 clinical disease models, over 140 genetically validated targets, and cGMP AAV manufacturing at 1,000L scale.

Additionally, TNYA's technology includes cellular regeneration and genetic modulation. This research explores how to convert one type of cell into a more useful form. Alternatively, they can shift diseased cells toward a healthier state. For instance, TNYA can potentially convert supporting heart cells into new cardiomyocytes, or nudge damaged heart muscle cells toward a more functional state. TNYA's approach also corrects specific DNA letters in the patient's genome. This is more precise than classic CRISPR editing because it potentially can fix a disease-causing mutation in the gene itself. This means this method could be more durable and less disruptive than traditional gene modification.

Furthermore, TNYA's precision medicine and small molecules use traditional oral drugs (i.e., pills), which are designed with genetically informed pharmacology. TNYA's work on genetic cardiomyopathies and heart-cell biology helps to understand which pathways are stressed in cardiomyocytes. They also consider how cells mishandle components related to their internal equilibrium (homeostasis) and where TNYA's drug candidates can potentially fit within that cellular network. So, instead of focusing on altering DNA structures, TNYA's approach changes how cells behave at a different level by modulating pathways that are disturbed/damaged.

Therefore, TNYA's pipeline centers around three main programs. The most important one is TN-201, which is a gene therapy for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). They also have TN-401 in Phase 1b/2 as a genetic therapy for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC). And lastly, TN-301 is a small-molecule HDAC6 inhibitor in a Phase 1b dose-escalation trial for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). TN-301 also completed a dose escalation test in healthy volunteers and is ready to initiate a Phase 1b/2a trial. On top of that, TNYA has other preclinical programs in monogenic cardiomyopathies and more common heart conditions, but I wouldn't consider them major value drivers at this R&D stage.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, TNYA trades at a $221.5 million market cap. Their latest 10-Q report for Q3 shows their balance sheet holds $56.3 million in cash against no financial debt (other than regular operating liabilities). Their book value reached $82.9 million, indicating a P/B of 2.7, which is slightly lower than its peers' P/B of 2.9. However, aside from that, TNYA has no revenues and recorded an EBIT loss of $20.9 million in Q3 2025.

I also estimate their nine-month cash burn (as of Q3 2025) reached $54.9 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX over that period. And if we annualize that number, it implies a yearly cash burn rate of approximately $73.2 million. This also translates to a suggested cash runway of just 0.8 years, which is relatively concerning. In my view, this alone raises TNYA's risk profile significantly, as another equity raise is likely in the near term (unless they issue debt instead).

Plus, it's worth highlighting that a year's worth of cash runway would be roughly equivalent to 33.1% of their current market cap. So, even a somewhat small equity raise would be highly dilutive for shareholders. However, note that this broadly matches management's own cash runway projection into 2H2026. Based on their anticipated milestones, my impression is that they're banking on reporting positive data on RIDGE-1's cohort 2, plus additional updates on MyPEAK-1 and RIDGE-1 by 1H2026.

Though, as you might imagine, this is inherently highly speculative for investors. After all, if the market doesn't reprice the shares higher, then any potential equity raises would be highly dilutive. Moreover, TN-201 and TN-401 are still in relatively early clinical stages. This adds yet another risk layer because TNYA has to prove the durability of its genetic treatment efficacy and long-term safety.

On top of that, the FDA has put a clinical hold on TN-201. This is probably one of the biggest setbacks for TNYA, even though the company believes its timelines won't be affected by this. Basically, this hold may cause short-term delays, but TNYA frames it as a manageable situation. This clinical hold is not about regulators demanding new preclinical programs or flagging toxicities. Instead, it's related to tightening trial protocols, which I anticipate will (at the very least) extend TNYA's timelines, even if it's ultimately manageable.

For context, a clinical hold means the FDA told TNYA to pause the trial until certain questions are answered or changes are made. Concretely, TNYA has to show how immune responses to AAV9 factor in the benefit-risk equation for patients. That's why the FDA wants higher standardization in the TN-201 MyPEAK-1 trial. The FDA asked TNYA to standardize patient monitoring in a way that the same rules are applied across all labs that check the same biomarkers. The FDA also wants TNYA to standardize how immunosuppression is managed across all trial locations.

Naturally, the bull case is that TNYA will manage to comply with the FDA's standardization requirements. And that TNYA's upcoming update on RIDGE-1 and MyPEAK-1 by 1H2026 will be positive as well. If those conditions are met, it's possible the market may turn more optimistic on their research, giving them room for a raise under more favorable terms. Plus, their runway seems to be sufficient to see them through those potential catalysts.

Conclusion: Neutral For Now

Overall, TNYA's underlying science is intriguing but still in early clinical stages. Their most advanced program is MyPEAK-1 in Phase 1b/2a, which means that even under the most optimistic assumptions, TNYA is still years away from any FDA approvals. Similarly, TNYA's runway is tight, and they've seen some regulatory setbacks with their recent clinical hold on TN-201. It's also a thinly traded microcap with only 2.1 million shares in volume, which, at the current $1.40 PPS, implies a shallow liquidity of $2.9 million. Thus, even small buy/sell orders can push the price. That's why, on balance, I feel TNYA's prospects are highly uncertain at this stage. Hence, I think TNYA is a "Hold" for now, but it's worth adding it to a watchlist until they shore up their balance sheet.

data.title: Mastercard: Becoming A Data Analytics Business
data.description:
Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V) have underperformed the overall market this year. While investors are still in the green, the S&P 500 (SPY) has performed 3 to 5 times better than Mastercard or Visa. The Magnificent 7 (MAGS) has 11x the return compared to Mastercard. However, on a forward P/E basis, Mastercard's multiple has contracted 10% to 15% year to date. I believe Mastercard might be a good buy at the current levels, and I will be adding shares to my portfolio. Mastercard is a blue-chip sleep-well-at-night (SWAN) business with growth catalysts in the future.

Recent Earnings Performance

Mastercard reported quarterly results on 10/30/2025. The company announced very strong earnings, as usual. Quarterly revenue came in at $8.6B, a new all-time record high, and ahead of analyst expectations at $8.53B. Revenue growth stood at a strong 17% year-over-year (or 15% on a constant currency basis), with the Payment Network net revenue growing 12% and the Value-Added Services rising to 25% (more on that later). It's really impressive that a company of this size (around $500B in market cap, with trailing twelve-month revenues of $31.474B) can continue to grow their top line in the high double-digit range. Not only that, but Mastercard is a very profitable company. Management announced $4.38 of EPS for the quarter, also ahead of analysts' expected $4.32. Notice how the operating margin stands at 58.8%, above the 54.3% in Q3/2024. The profit margin and free cash flow margin are also elevated, oscillating near 45% and 54%, respectively, in the most recent quarters.

The company runs very efficiently and at full steam. Management has been able to grow top lines at 17% year-over-year while keeping the operating expenses low. In fact, these have only grown modestly, near the mid-single digits. One can only note minor increases in spending in G&A and Advertising & Marketing. This is an "A+" type of an income statement.

Mastercard does not need to spend a lot of money on marketing and advertising. Their credit card business is one of the largest in terms of total market share or credit cards issued. This company has very strong brand recognition, only rivaled by Visa (V) in this segment.

There is more. Mastercard currently has $10.313B in cash and cash equivalents and $335M in investments. The company has no short-term debt and $18.98B in long-term debt, approximately. And while this might seem a large number, one must not forget that this company generated $12.65B in cash from operations and $5.49B in free cash flow in just one quarter. On a trailing twelve-month basis, Mastercard has generated over $17B in free cash flow. The debt structure is reasonable, too. There's $1.938B of debt due (10Q, page 18) in 2027, $2.75B in 2028, $1.88B in 2029, and the rest due in 2030 or later. Not much compared to the cash they hold and generate.

This is a company that rewards shareholders through a $3/year dividend and a generous share buyback program (the company has bought back around 10% of its shares since 2020). The company has been raising their dividend at 15% per year, approximately. Yet, their payout ratio is only 19%. And that's not it. Management does not dilute much shareholder value through stock-based compensation. On a trailing twelve month basis, the stock-based compensation has been $593M. That's compared to over $17B in free cash flow. I believe this to be more than reasonable.

In short, this company keeps beating numbers, has a strong moat and a business model that is difficult to disrupt, management is friendly to its shareholders, has a juggernaut of a balance sheet and profit conversion strategy, and has found a way to pivot and find more potentially high-growth opportunities that leverage their huge amounts of data. That's the Value-Added Services.

Mastercard's Value-Added Services

Let me start by stating that I love this side of Mastercard's business. This segment is growing at double the rate (25% year-over-year) compared to their well-known Payment Network (12% year-over-year increase) operations. During Q1/2022, the company reported a value-added services quarterly revenue of $1.768B. As of Q3/2025, the quarterly revenue for the segment has almost doubled, reaching $3.423B.

The company has sustained very elevated growth rates for the value-added services side, consistently above their Payment Network. While the latter has oscillated between 8 and 13% during the past years, the value-added service side has ranged between 16% and 20%. Not only that, but the revenue growth rates have been accelerating in the past quarters, reaching 25% in their most recent quarter (organic growth would be slightly slower, as the acquisition of Recorded Future and Minna increased the VAS revenue growth by 3 points).

There is no doubt that this side of the business is very strong. However, what exactly is value-added services? In the past few years, Mastercard's management has been pivoting to offer additional services to clients. Value-added services include everything from offerings related to cybersecurity & intelligence, data & other services, and processing & payment infrastructure. The segment is the response from management to continue innovating, disrupting, and finding additional growth levers. This company is expanding beyond the payments processing business and is slowly becoming a data aggregator and data analytics company that happens to have one of the largest payment networks. And that's the beauty of it. Both sides of the business benefit from each other in a very strong symbiotic relationship.

The value-added services commercializes specialized tools, data, and consulting to private businesses, banks, and governments. Mastercard leverages a huge amount of data, consumer habits, and other metadata that the company is involved with as part of their payments network segment. This is especially powerful given that Mastercard is the second largest credit card company by market share (they process huge amounts of transactions every day!). The value-added services segment can be classified into three categories, mainly:

Security and Identity Solutions

The company leverages the vast amounts of data that Mastercard processes by providing services to secure transactions, prevent fraud, and manage other cyber risks. The company does not only use their knowledge and expertise to cover themselves from fraud losses from their credit card network. Instead, Mastercard sells these services to clients such as financial institutions, payment processors, retailers, merchants, etc., becoming an important partner. Mastercard offers RiskRecon, Mastercard Identity Check, Mastercard Risk Decisioning, Ethoca Alerts, Brighterion AI and AI Network Solutions.

The company highly leverages AI and Machine Learning models to offer a high value and desirable proposition. The Decision Intelligence AI model is able to understand billions of transactions and assign risk scores to every payment, with the AI flagging suspicious payments. Their AI models can also proactively block credit cards that have been potentially compromised by screening suspicious sites and the dark web. Besides the purchasing and transaction data, Mastercard also offers services where the behavioral biometrics are analyzed (for instance, typing rhythm) to prevent scamming and fraud. In a world where cybersecurity and fraud prevention are increasingly worrying many companies, Mastercard is there to provide their solutions and benefit from increased spending in this category.

Data and Insights

The vast amounts of data that flow through Mastercard's network are also transformed and utilized to produce consulting advice and concrete and tailored business intelligence. Mastercard helps customers with pricing, marketing, and investing. It can provide companies with very detailed and specific information about the market and consumer behavior and trends. The company typically offers this service to retailers, grocery stores, restaurants, financial institutions, and governments through SpendingPulse, Test & Learn, or Mastercard Advisors.

Mastercard offers services that utilize AI models to measure impacts of potential pricing changes and promotions, empowering their customers with more information to before any decision is made and any change is rolled out. Their Market Intelligence division also utilizes AI models to analyze global spending data and predict future trends, significantly helping retailers. Think of businesses looking for the next best spot to open the next restaurant, or to understand if they can increase the price of certain items without losing customers. This is, indeed, a very attractive proposition.

Consumer Engagement

Another way to utilize the data is to entice and help drive credit card usage and spending. Mastercard is able to build long-term relationships with customers with personalized loyalty programs and rewards. For instance, the company offers their SessionM Loyalty Platform, Dynamic Yield, other Marketing Services as consultant, and the Loyalty Digital Enabler.

Mastercard provides value by ensuring that every customer can see a relevant recommendation on a website or app, offering a more tailored user experience. Obviously, AI is behind this in their Dynamic Yield product.

Overall, I am very bullish on this side of Mastercard's business. It has been proven to be desirable and fast-growing, and I believe it will continue to outperform their payments network business in the future.

Valuation

Mastercard does not trade at a "cheap" valuation when one only looks at the P/E ratio. On a forward P/E basis, the company trades at 28.6, above Visa's 22.84. However, Mastercard also grows faster than Visa. While Visa has grown their top line near the 10% mark in the recent future, Mastercard grows at 15% approximately, and the revenue growth seems to be accelerating. One might be inclined to believe that a 28.6 P/E for Mastercard is expensive, given that the market currently trades at a forward P/E of 22.6 (or 28.2 for the information technology sector). However, Mastercard is one of the best-quality businesses of the overall market, and I believe it deserves a premium valuation (see, for instance, other high-quality businesses like Costco (COST), Microsoft (MSFT), or Amazon (AMZN)). This is no different than paying higher prices for other high-quality items that we buy on a daily basis.

Historically, Mastercard has been trading at 30 to 45 TTM price-to-earnings (market in orange in the top plot). Currently, it trades at a forward earnings multiple of 28.6. In addition, Mastercard has also been trading at 24 to 26 times its free cash flow. And it is currently trading at 29.5.

For these reasons, I believe that it is a good time to be adding Mastercard at these levels. I do not believe this is a "steal deal" or "free money", but I do believe that I am paying a fair price for a wonderful company.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Obviously, there are risks in investing in Mastercard.

For once, let's discuss the valuation first. Mastercard trades at a higher valuation compared to the market because of its great quality (again, wonderful company at a fair price). However, if the company started disappointing investors by missing on revenue and EPS on its quarterly reports, Wall Street might readjust how they feel about the company deserving a premium. And even though I do not believe this will happen, investors should be aware of the possibility.

It is also possible that government regulations regarding how Mastercard makes money by facilitating smooth transactions impact the company's bottom line, reducing their profitability. Governments in Europe or the United States could limit and more tightly regulate interchange fees, which would be detrimental for Mastercard's Payment Network business.

Finally, its Value-Added Services growth could start decelerating. Mastercard could face increased competition from other cybersecurity and fraud detection providers, for instance. However, I believe the company is in a unique position, as almost no company in the world has access to the vast amounts of transactional data that Mastercard has, which enables them to build better fraud detection models and provide more tailored consulting services.

Conclusion

Mastercard is a blue-chip sleep-well-at-night type of business. It is continuously growing, and I like the management's pivot to become a data analysis and data aggregator company. I believe there is plenty of runway ahead for this company. In addition, Mastercard currently trades at a reasonable valuation, and I will gladly pay a fair price for a great company.

data.title: 6 Reasons To Buy Sprouts Farmers Market Stock
data.description:
Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) stock has fallen from grace, more than 50% off its June 2025 all-time high. The stock used to be richly valued, so I'm not entirely surprised at the drop, but it's worth analyzing whether the drop is overdone or not. I think it is. A big part of the sell-off came after its most recent Q3 earnings results, which missed revenue expectations and came with lowered guidance.

However, I think there are six reasons to consider buying SFM stock. The long-term thesis still looks intact, as Sprouts Farmers Market is a fundamentally strong company (strong balance sheet, profitability, and cash flow) in a growing market. Further, SFM can bounce back in the short term due to aggressive buybacks and an oversold stock. Not to mention, despite lowered expectations, analysts still expect a good amount of growth going forward, and a new loyalty program can help with that growth. The cherry on top is that the valuation is quite reasonable.

I rate SFM stock a Buy. That being said, it's a cautious Buy, as we'll need to see how the recent headwinds (weakening consumer) play out in the next couple of quarters.

What Caused The Q3 Sell-Off In SFM Stock?

As most readers likely already know, Sprouts' Q3 results disappointed the market, as revenue missed expectations and guidance was lowered. The stock finished 26.1% lower on October 30 as a result. I'll give a quick recap of the Q3 highlights.

First, GAAP EPS was $1.22, which actually beat estimates by $0.05. However, revenue of $2.2 billion grew 13% year-over-year but slightly missed expectations by $20 million. That's not a bad miss, so what caused the drop?

Well, one culprit was the diluted EPS guidance of $0.86-0.90 compared to the $0.98 consensus estimate. As a result, the full-year EPS guidance of $5.24-5.28 missed the $5.31 consensus. Moreover, revenue guidance suggests around 14% growth for the year, but the consensus estimate was 15.15%, and management's previous guidance was for a growth rate of 14.5-16%. Next, comparable sales growth of 7% is expected for the full year instead of management's previous estimate of 7.5-9%.

The reasons management gave for the performance were challenging year-over-year comps and signs of a weakening consumer. Specifically, CEO & Director Jack Sinclair said the following:

While it was a solid third quarter, it fell short of our top line expectations. As the quarter progressed, our comp sales moderated faster than expected as we came up against challenging year-on-year comparisons as well as signs of a softening consumer.

But Here's Why SFM Is Worth Considering Now

There are a few reasons to consider SFM stock. Let's talk about the reasons one by one.

#1: Sprouts Farmers Market offers a wide variety of differentiated products in a growing "niche" grocery space.

Sprouts doesn't cater to everyday consumers but rather more health-conscious consumers, which is a growing market. According to Grand View Research, "The health and wellness foods market in the U.S. was estimated at USD 250 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow at a CAGR of 9.4% over the forecast period." One thing about operating in this market is that the company doesn't have to compete on price in the same way Walmart (NYSE:WMT) does.

Sprouts' customers are generally less price-sensitive (although still somewhat price-sensitive, let's not fool ourselves), as they don't mind paying extra for something that is healthier. I speak from experience because I am, for the most part, this type of grocery shopper. A key point is that this leaves Sprouts Farmers Market with higher profit margins, which I'll discuss next.

#2: The company's profitability, growth, cash flow, and balance sheet are still in good shape.

Profitability: I find it very impressive that SFM has a return on equity of 37% in the past 12 months. It's more impressive when you realize that the company achieved that figure with minimal debt, as it only has $40.87 million worth of debt compared to $1.435 billion of equity. Some companies have high ROEs because they operate with large amounts of debt, but not SFM.

In fact, SFM has managed to increase its return on equity over the years while lowering its debt levels. You can see the trend of increasing equity and lower debt below.

ROE is one of many metrics investors can look at, but it's quite an important one that successful investors like Warren Buffett love. SFM's ROE gets an A+ grade on Seeking Alpha, as it's higher than the sector median of 11.39% and higher than SFM's five-year average of 29.2%.

Growth: The company's growth rate is also still good, despite the lowered guidance. This is especially true for a grocery chain. Looking past 2025 (where EPS is expected to grow by ~40%), analysts expect 9.43% and 12.17% for 2026 and 2027, respectively. Also, revenue growth for 2026 and 2027 is expected to be ~10% and 9.32%, respectively. Those estimates aren't bad, by any means, especially when combined with a reasonable valuation.

#3: Aggressive buybacks at a depressed share price will boost EPS.

Another potential catalyst is the buyback program. Sprouts has a $1 billion share repurchase authorization with $966 million left on it as of the earnings call on October 29. That's 11.6% of its $8.32 billion market cap, and I'd be willing to bet that management took advantage of the drop after earnings since Curtis Valentine, the company's CFO, stated, "We expect to continue repurchasing shares opportunistically." The company has enough cash (~$322 million) and cash flow to repurchase a meaningful number of shares.

Interestingly, management's full-year EPS expectations of $5.24-5.28 don't include any additional share repurchases. That means management is setting itself up to beat expectations, as a lower share count will boost EPS.

#4. The stock is oversold.

Any oversold stock can bounce back simply because it's oversold, so that's what SFM stock has in its favor. However, we can't ignore the short-term resistance level in the mid-$86 area. I'd want to see the stock close above ~$87 before getting more bullish. I'd also be wary of holding if it breaks the $76 support area, which has held its ground twice so far. A break would tell us that not even aggressive buybacks are enough to support the stock.

Nonetheless, I think a break above $87 can lead us to $100 or higher to fill the gap in the chart from the earnings report. If the gap gets filled, that would represent 21%+ upside.

#5. The new loyalty program has the potential to boost sales in the next few months.

If the consumer truly is weakening, then loyalty programs can help keep them around and spend more. On October 29, during the earnings call, management mentioned that the Sprouts Rewards loyalty program launched that week. When speaking about the program, Jack Sinclair stated:

Although it's still early in the program, we are observing encouraging indications of increased shopping frequency and sales per customer in our early rollout geographies and are excited to see our progress in the coming months.

This is not a guaranteed fix, but it could help.

#6: The stock is reasonably valued.

Importantly, the stock is now reasonably valued. Based on analysts' estimates from the screenshot in point #2, the forward P/E for this year is 16.1x. Looking forward to next year, the forward P/E drops to 14.7x. For reference, its five-year average forward P/E is 19.1x.

Also, its TTM P/E ratio is 16.4x, below the five-year average trailing non-GAAP P/E is 19.7x. Even 19.7x seems like a very reasonable valuation for a relatively stable grocery company with growth ahead.

If the company were to achieve the expected $5.76 in EPS for 2026 and have even an 18x P/E multiple by the end of next year (roughly 14-15 months from now by the time the Q4-2026 earnings report is out), then the share price would be $103.68. That would signal a 21.8% increase in about 1.25 years. It's not bad. It's also not a crazy multiple to assume.

The Risks

Investing in SFM stock is not without its risks. It's a volatile stock, as you already know. Also, Q3's disappointment could be the start of a multi-quarter trend of disappointments, so that's something I'll be watching out for now. Up until recently, EPS was generally being revised higher, but Q3 marked a change in that trend.

Another risk is that we don't have enough confirmation that the stock has bottomed yet. It's oversold, but it hasn't quite "bottomed out" in the traditional technical sense. At least not in my opinion. As stated earlier, $76 is a key support area to keep your eyes on.

Finally, the stock's performance will partially depend on the health of the consumer they target, which is mainly out of the company's control. If you're not bullish on the middle-income consumer, then you may want to consider a different stock.

The Bottom Line On SFM Stock

Sprouts Farmers Market is a solid company with a consistently strong return on equity, respectable growth ahead, and a healthy balance sheet. Following the share price decline, I see an opportunity to buy an oversold stock of a good company at a reasonable price, and management will almost surely take advantage of the depressed share price to buy back shares and boost EPS, which can support the stock price.

Still, due to the risks mentioned above, like a weakening consumer and a stock that hasn't clearly bottomed out yet, I rate it as a cautious Buy.

data.title: Jacobs: Multi-Year Growth Driven By Strong Backlog And Increasing AI Infrastructure Demand
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Jacobs Solutions Inc. (J) in May with a buy rating, and the stock is up ~7% since then. Moving forward, the company's growth prospects look positive, and it is poised for multi-year revenue growth given the record $23.1 bn backlog, a book/bill above 1.1x, and significantly expanding pipelines in key end markets. The structural demand drivers, including AI-driven power and data center build-outs as well as reshoring in pharma and semiconductors, give strong visibility into continued top-line expansion.

The company should also continue to see margin acceleration as higher-margin backlog converts to revenue, global delivery from low-cost centers increases, and AI design tools and digital twin capabilities help in operational efficiencies and premium pricing.

In terms of valuation, the stock is near its historical averages. I see a potential for P/E to re-rate above historical averages given good multi-year revenue growth visibility and margin expansion potential. Hence, I continue to have a buy rating on the stock.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

In Q4 FY2025, Jacobs Solutions achieved solid financial performance with a 6.6% Y/Y increase in total gross revenues to $3.2 billion and a 5.8% Y/Y increase in adjusted net revenues (after deducting pass-through costs) to $2.2 billion. Critical Infrastructure business grew 9.2% Y/Y as a result of the ramp-up of transportation projects, and growth in the Energy and Power, and Life Sciences & Advanced Manufacturing saw a 5.3% Y/Y increase, helped by strength in data centers and life sciences. Water and Environmental revenue decreased by -1.1% Y/Y due to environmental softness in the U.S., offsetting the growth in the water end-market.

PA Consulting experienced strong financial performance as well, with a ~10% Y/Y increase in revenue, driven by an increasing demand for their services from the Public and National Security markets.

Looking forward, I'm positive about Jacobs' growth prospects. The company exited the last quarter with a solid backlog of $23.1 billion, providing strong visibility to support near-term revenue growth. Backlog is a leading indicator for Jacobs' business, and the company also has a book-to-bill of 1.1x, meaning that new orders are coming in faster than revenue is being recognized. This should continue to aid revenue growth going forward.

End-market-wise, all three end-markets: Life Sciences & Advanced Manufacturing, Water & Environmental, and Critical Infrastructure, should see healthy trends. In Life Sciences and Advanced Manufacturing, the management highlighted that the life sciences pipeline is higher by about 50%. The semiconductors and data centers are also seeing strong growth. There's a significant reshoring push in the US from pharma, semiconductor, and data center clients. On the pharma and semiconductor side, the government policy continues to be supportive of domestic investments. On the data center side, there is AI-related demand leading to another wave of infrastructure build-out.

Management noted that the pipeline for data centers was up 5x in just the last quarter. Jacobs is moving from its traditional white space designs to incorporate grey space infrastructure, behind-the-meter power needs, and reclaimed water solutions. A recent collaboration with NVIDIA that leverages AI-driven design and digital twinning for gigawatt-scale facilities strengthens the company's positioning. The opportunity set includes data centers, neo-cloud providers, and multi-tenant operators with demand in the US as well as Europe and the Middle East. On the whole, this end market is being driven by strong multi-year structural drivers.

In the Water and Environmental business, the water business pipeline was up 50% globally. That should support good growth in the coming years. In water, Jacobs' full-lifecycle delivery model and its AI-powered Aqua DNA platform provide Jacobs with the ability to develop higher-quality recurring revenue streams. On the environmental side, FY25 was softer as private sector clients slowed spending due to regulatory uncertainty, and U.S. public sector work was impacted by the shift of FEMA relief funding to the state level. As these issues are resolved, this business should return to growth in FY26 and beyond, which should boost the company's overall revenue trajectory.

Jacobs is also well-positioned to take advantage of growing demand for Critical Infrastructure, driven by significant transport investments (IIJA in the U.S.), large transport projects around the world, and strength in Energy & Power (e.g., AI-driven power requirements and electrification of grids). Large transport projects, such as the MTA Interborough Express light-rail line in New York and Jacobs' success with the Crown Commercial Service Framework in the U.K., position Jacobs in advisory and delivery roles across transportation and clean-energy investments. This should create long-term avenues for continued growth in this segment for Jacobs.

What I particularly like about Jacobs is that its demand strength is not limited to just the U.S. Projects in the Middle East, such as New Murabba Mukaab and Expo 2030 projects, along with potential rail/city building projects in Abu Dhabi and other parts of the Gulf Region, should benefit Jacobs. Europe is recovering as there is increased clarity around government budgets. Southeast Asia, Australia, and New Zealand are all seeing strong activity in transportation and water projects.

Further, in addition to good organic growth, the company's healthy balance sheet with net leverage of ~0.8x at the end of last quarter should enable it to do bolt-on M&As to complement organic growth. So, I remain optimistic about the company's growth prospects.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q4 FY2025, adjusted operating margin was up 130 bps Y/Y to 14.5%. Cost reduction initiatives and value-added services growth helped margins. In the I&AF segment, the adjusted operating margin of 13.2% was up 120 bps Y/Y. PA Consulting posted an adjusted operating margin of 22.6%, up 130 bps Y/Y. Margins benefited from managing costs, operating leverage, and early gains from the global delivery model. Investments in AI-enabled, high-value offerings and digital capabilities also helped margins.

Looking forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin prospects. The robust demand environment is providing Jacobs with strong pricing power, and management indicated that gross profit in its backlog for orders was up over 13% versus the prior year. As this higher-margin backlog becomes revenue in the coming years, it should help boost the company's margin profile.

The margins should also benefit from the ongoing rollout of the global delivery model. Today, about 38% of the total billable hours are from global delivery. Management aims to take this above 52% by using cheaper and good-quality delivery centers in the Philippines, India, and other such global hubs. The company should see a steady growth in operating margins as more work gets transferred to these centers.

Jacobs is also embedding artificial intelligence, digital twins, and automated technology into its delivery process to help reduce lead times and rework and deliver high-value results to customers. Using its AI technology, Jacobs has been able to charge its customers premium prices for project work and move to a higher level of technology-based areas of work, which has a higher margin business.

Overall, I remain optimistic about the company's margin prospects.

Valuation and Ratings

Jacobs currently trades at 19.61x FY26 consensus EPS of $7.07, which is in line with its 5-year average forward P/E of 19.47x. On FY27 consensus EPS, the stock is trading at a discount.

From a fundamental view, the company is performing very well and has good multi-year revenue growth visibility as well as margin expansion prospects. I believe the stock deserves to trade at a premium to its historical level. Solid backlog, a book-to-bill above 1, and a growing pipeline of projects in attractive sectors, such as the data center, life sciences, and water sectors, make revenue growth prospects favorable. The margin outlook also looks attractive, given the scaling of global delivery channels and AI-enabled design tools. The margins in the backlog are already higher, and we should see continued margin gains in the coming quarters.

Even if we assume that the stock continues to trade at its current P/E, the double-digit EPS growth over the next few years should result in a nice amount of upside. So, I have a buy rating.

Risks

While the end-market looks very strong, Jacobs still needs to execute well from an operations perspective. If there are cost overruns on contracts, delays on projects, or problems in the ramp-up of global delivery, then Jacobs could experience an increase in earnings volatility and disappoint investors.

Moreover, if there is a decline in client spending, particularly in those market areas that are dependent on government spending/incentives or are highly regulated, such as environmental work, then the company's business may be adversely impacted.

data.title: ETHA: Ethereum's Stablecoin Story Is Improving
data.description:
It has been a couple of months since I've provided a monthly recap for Ethereum (ETH-USD) via Seeking Alpha. Specifically for the iShares Ethereum ETF (ETHA), you'd have to go back all the way to March to find my most recent piece. Back then, I focused primarily on the organic bid for Ethereum as the gas token of the cryptocurrency market. I concluded that despite positive signs in usage, the lack of fees was a potential problem for price:

Like many others who buy digital assets, my view has generally been that ETH is the 'digital gas' of the cryptocurrency market. With this view in mind, it's important for the fundamental thesis that the network generates fees. This keeps an organic bid on the token and also creates the burn mechanism, which reduces inflation. Thus, Ethereum's quickly eroding fee market even as secondary layers scale the network is making it increasingly difficult to justify ETH as an investment at this point in time.

As fate would have it, ETHA has rallied more than 40% since that article. In this update, we'll again look at Ethereum's network usage. To be sure, there are positive signs there. We'll also look at the capital flow story since it is such an important part of what is driving demand for the token. And we'll get into valuation of the asset relative to fundamentals.

Network Activity Through November 2025

In many ways, November 2025 was a continuation of a longer-term trend that we've witnessed on the Ethereum blockchain going back to the 'Dencun' upgrade. Network fees continue to go down as the average user fee paid for each transaction continues its slide down to just 51 cents per:

November 2024 October 2025 November 2025 YoY MoM
DAAs (000s) 400.5 490.6 483.7 20.8% -1.4%
Transactions 36.7 48 45.7 24.5% -4.8%
Fees $191.60 $41.40 $23.60 -87.7% -43.0%
Avg Tx Fee $5.15 $0.84 $0.51 -90.1% -39.3%

Source: Token Terminal, Transactions and Fees in millions

Year-over-year, average transaction fees are down by 90%, which is very much in line with the decline in total network fees at 88%. The good news is daily active addresses hit 483k in the month. This was a 21% year-over-year increase, though a slight dip from October. Transactions hit 45.7 million in November; again, a noticeable 24.5% increase from November last year. DAAs and transactions each hit record highs back in August but have since pulled back slightly. Still, both metrics are above long term averages. Aiding this surge in transactions has been Ethereum's continued dominance in the stablecoin market.

At the end of November, Ethereum accounted for over $171 billion of the market's $304 billion total supply. More importantly, Ethereum is seeing its share of stablecoin volume grow:

Stable Tx Vol ($b) November 2024 October 2025 November 2025 YoY MoM
Total Market $2,900 $7,300 $5,700 96.6% -21.9%
Ethereum $858 $2,700 $2,500 191.2% -7.4%
ETH Share 22.84% 27.00% 30.49% 33.5% 12.9%

Source: Artemis

Where companies like Circle Internet Group (CRCL) benefit from growth in stablecoin supply, Ethereum generated revenue from the actual usage of those coins, not from the yields from T-bill collateral. In November, Ethereum's $2.5 trillion in stable transaction volume was good for 30.5% of the total market. This was 33.5% year-over-year growth in transaction volume share. And it isn't just Ethereum that has seen this kind of growth:

Layer 2 networks like Base have also seen their share of stablecoin transaction volume grow year over year, from 18.7% last November to 22.5% this November. Base is actually now the second largest blockchain network by stablecoin transaction volume.

DAT & ETF Flows

Usage growth aside, capital flows have generally been the biggest driver of returns in the digital asset market since the approval of spot ETFs last year. Compared with other assets in the market, ETH's share of capital flows in 2025 has been stellar:

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) -$2,810 $26,783 $142,662
Ethereum -$1,397 $12,893 $25,512
Multi-asset $37.2 $1 $7,104
Solana (SOL-USD) $101.7 $3,389 $3,454
XRP (XRP-USD) $785.4 $2,890 $3,129
Total -$3,223 $46,105 $183,345
BTC Dominance 87.2% 58.1% 77.8%
ETH Dominance 43.3% 28.0% 13.9%

Source: CoinShares, Bloomberg as of 11/28/25

While Ethereum's 28% share of 2025 investment flows is far from Bitcoin's 58%, it is a large improvement over the 13.9% share of AUM. November was a difficult month for both assets, with a combined outflow of over $4 billion between the two. Lower market cap tokens did a bit better. From a DAT perspective, November was a month of holdings growth, though that growth has slowed considerably over the last several months.

At the end of November, ETH DATs held 6.4 million ETH, which was up slightly from the 6.1 million ETH at the end of October. However, this didn't quite make up for the roughly 425k ETH that came out of the ETFs. Combined DAT and ETH supply has been generally flat since mid-October.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, we have a clear case of multiple expansion judging by the circulating price-to-fees ratio:

At just under 1,400x fees, Ethereum is almost as expensive as it has ever been. I personally like the P/F ratio because I see it as a reasonable comp for an equity price to sales multiple. In the past, we've seen the coin grow into these types of extended multiples. The fact that usage is growing is a good sign. But still, ETH is not 'cheap' at these levels.

Network DeFi TVL MCap/TVL
Ethereum $71.0b 5.4
Solana $9.2b 8.7
BSC (BNB-USD) $7.1b 17.5

Source: DeFiLlama

That said, viewing the coin through a DeFi TVL ratio tells a slightly different story. At $71 billion in TVL, Ethereum's market cap to TVL ratio is just 5.4. This is much lower than peer L1 networks and leaves the valuation interpretation up to each individual investor's preference. To be clear, I like fees better because I see Ethereum as essentially a settlement layer for digital commerce and tokenization. Thus, fees are an important input for Ethereum's fundamentals.

iShares Ethereum ETF

ETHA continues to be the monster in the Ethereum ETF market.

At 48% of the market supply, ETHA has been the clear winner in the Ethereum ETF game to this point. The fund's share of volume is even higher at roughly 70%. At just 25 basis points, ETHA is among the cheapest spot ETH ETFs in the market. However, unlike Grayscale's flagship ETH fund, ETHA has not yet been cleared by the SEC for asset staking. Until that happens, it's difficult for me to call the fund a better 'buy' than funds that do offer staking yields.

Closing Summary

I continue to believe ETH is an important part of the broader blockchain ecosystem, and any digital asset portfolio should include an allocation to the token and/or proxies of the token. While I'm certainly not of the view that Ethereum is cheap at 1,400x fees, history shows that ETH generally grows into its valuation through usage growth. So far, we're seeing growth in transactions and users in 2025. Yet, the fees are not quite there. The network has a sizable lead in stablecoins, which I view as another reason to be long the token in some way. While I like other opportunities better than ETHA specifically, assuming the fund is allowed to enable staking at some point in the future, it is definitely one to consider for long-term holding.

data.title: Turkcell: Pain Today For More Prosperity Tomorrow
data.description:
I turned neutral on Turkcell (TKC) at the start of this year, concerned about management's strategic priorities, ongoing macroeconomic challenges in Türkiye, fierce competition in the Turkish mobile and fixed-line services markets, and the risk of meaningful capex spending increases.

Those issues have all come home to roost to varying degrees this year, leading the ADRs to a roughly 14% decline since my last update and underperformance relative to rivals like Turk Telekom (OTCPK:TRKNY) and Vodafone (VOD), other emerging market service providers like MTN Group (OTCPK:MTNOY), and the iShares MSCI Turkey ETF (TUR).

The reset in price and valuation makes this a more interesting situation, particularly in light of 2025, which has seen the company continue to maintain a leading share in Türkiye while generating better-than-expected margins and continuing to grow promising businesses like fiber services and mobile payments. The "but" is that the company is looking at major capex spending in the coming years to support a 5G rollout - a "pain today, prosper tomorrow" opportunity that will eventually improve profits and margins further.

Given how these capex overhangs have played out in the past, it seems early to call an "all clear", but it is a tempting name to monitor for further pullbacks.

A Better-Than-Expected Quarter

Turkcell continues to leverage best-in-class service quality and substantial investments in its mobile and fiber ecosystem to drive ongoing share growth in high-value postpaid subscribers while also maintaining ARPU growth comfortably ahead of Türkiye's still-problematic inflation. That, in turn, continues to drive some of the best reported margins the company has seen.

Revenue rose 11% in the third quarter, with core mobile service revenue up 11%. Consumer revenue (up about 11%) remains the large majority of the service business, but Corporate in seeing some decent growth (up 17%), helped by strong growth in data center and cloud services (up almost 51%).

Underpinning the service revenue growth, Turkcell saw modest overall subscriber growth (up less than 1% yoy and qoq), but 7% year-over-year growth in post-paid subs and strong growth in average revenue per user (or ARPU) of almost 10%, with adjusted post-paid ARPU growth of 11.5% (and these post-paid subs pay about 2.5x more on average per month than prepaid subs).

Turkcell also continues to see solid growth in its fiber business, with fiber subs up 4% yoy (to 2.5M versus 30.6M post-paid mobile subs) and residential fiber ARPU up 17%. At this point the main limiting factor in growing this business is access - the company's capture rate is over 42%, but they only pass around 6.2M homes at this point.

The company's tech-driven payments business continues to grow nicely as well, with overall Techfin revenue up 20% and Paycell revenue up 42% on 78% transaction volume growth.

EBITDA grew almost 11% this quarter, beating by more than 4% (as per Visible Alpha), with margin declining 30 bp to 43.9%. Turkcell Türkiye EBITDA rose 11% with margin up 10 bp to 45.7%, while Techfin saw EBITDA down 6% (margin down six points to 21.9%) due to a significant revenue mix shift toward lower-margin point-of-sale transactions as Türkiye's central bank previously okayed larger limits on mobile transactions.

With these results, management raised guidance for the remainder of the year, with revenue expected to grow 10% (versus 7% to 8% previously) and EBITDA margin of 42% to 43%.

Competition Has Been Rougher Of Late

While Turkcell's market share has been relatively stable in the low-40%s for quite some time, that may suggest a false impression of calmness in the market. In truth, Turk Telekom has been pushing hard to grow its business, outgrowing Turkcell in four of the last seven quarters (just missing another win in Q3), though with Turkcell typically enjoying better margins.

This competition has arguably put some limits on what Turkcell can do with pricing and has perhaps constrained subscriber growth somewhat. That said, it is Vodafone that has seen the bigger impact, with Turk Telekom passing Vodafone to become the #2 player with a 30% share (after quite a while in third place).

While the change in competition is more evident in the mobile services business, the fiber business has been highly competitive as well, with Turk Telekom enjoying a huge incumbent advantage in homes passed with its services (over 33 million, including close to 15 million with their most advanced fiber offerings).

I'm not too concerned about the longer-term ramifications here. Turk Telekom hasn't been behaving irresponsibly, and I don't see much risk of them "poisoning the well" for everybody. If anything, I think it's Vodafone that has the bigger issue now, as they seem less and less relevant in the post-paid space.

Spending Money To Make Money

The reasons why a stock has moved the way it has aren't always obvious, and I can appreciate that some investors may be frustrated by the dichotomy between Turkcell's strong reported operating performance and the share price performance.

The issue, I believe, is the impact of a major upcoming investment cycle.

Turkcell is moving forward with 5G, having won significant spectrum at auction in October and looking to launch in 2026. It's not as if the Street didn't know this was coming at some point, but now we know that the company will be paying $1.47B for 42% of the available 5G spectrum (the maximum it's allowed to own), with Turk Telekom getting around 35% and Vodafone getting the rest.

Although the 5G auctions were a known event, they brought in about 40% more than expected, and at least some analysts and investors are worried about the risk of "winner's curse" and Turkcell overpaying for capacity, not to mention the looming outlays for the actual 5G hardware. I'd also note that Turkcell extended its rights to 2G-4.5G spectrum through 2042, agreeing to pay 5% of gross mobile service revenue annually starting in 2029.

This isn't the first time this has happened; the market freaked out about the price Turkcell paid for 4G spectrum back in 2015, but I'd argue that those purchases have served the company well over the past decade as the company's leading 4G/4.5G offerings have made a significant difference in growing ARPU and facilitating ventures like mobile payments.

To be sure, capex spending will accelerate in the coming years, and I expect there will be concerns about whether or not the company can and will maintain (let alone grow) its dividend during that period. While dividend growth may indeed be tempered or curtailed for a bit, Turkcell has the borrowing capacity to not only fund its likely capex needs, but also maintain its dividend without destabilizing the balance sheet.

The Outlook

Modeling a company's financials through periods of high inflation is no simple task, and while inflation has eased some lately, a four-year low of 31% isn't exactly "back to normal". To that end, the outlook gets very hazy beyond the next couple of years. I do expect strong revenue growth over the next two years (in the mid-20%s), and I believe the launch of 5G services will have a significant positive impact on revenue and margins (as it has for many service providers). Longer term, I expect revenue growth to slow to a point where the long-term growth rate comes out in the mid-teens, but again, actual inflation will have a lot of impact here.

On the margin/cash flow side, I do expect Turkcell to maintain EBITDA margins in the low-to-mid-40%s for a few years, with more leverage coming down the line as 5G ramps and matures and as businesses like Paycell continue to grow. Long term, I still think mid-teens free cash flow margins are possible, driving over 20% annualized FCF growth (again, highly subject to inflation).

Discounting back, I get a fair value of around $7.50 for the ADRs today, with the main change from my prior target being more exact timing on the cash flow impacts of the 5G capex. Fundamentally, not much has really changed in my modeling assumptions. While I do like multiples-based valuation as a backup/backstop to discounted cash flows, it's tricky here. The market has been imposing a hefty discount on Turkish stocks; while a typical EBITDA multiple here would arguably be something in the 4x-5x range, the low end of that range drives a $13 fair value that just doesn't seem realistic now. Likewise with P/E, as unsustainable near-term EPS growth distorts that sort of approach.

The Bottom Line

With the share price and the business operating performance diverging some this year, Turkcell is more interesting to me than it was at the start of the year. I still have some concerns about management (including an arguably too-cozy relationship with the government), but I like the 5G strategy, and I'm still bullish on the potential for data- and payment-driven growth in the coming years, as well as the potential to continue reaping healthy margins. Today's price is tempting, but given my concerns about Türkiye's economy and corporate governance, I'm inclined to hold out for a bit better prospect return today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NIE: Reliable Dividends While Maintaining Exposure To Technology
data.description:
Overview

I believe that Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NIE) is a unique closed-end fund because it provides instant exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world while offering a high dividend yield. The underlying strategy may be a bit complex, but the fund has a long track record of success. When I previously covered NIE, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation. While I still believe that NIE is a great buy, I wanted to revisit the fund to specifically discuss some of the performance tradeoffs for the fund and how it's best utilized by investors.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NIE's share price has increased by about 3.8%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive momentum of the market over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 12.9% over the same time frame. NIE now offers investors a starting yield of 7.8%, and the fund has a long history of maintaining consistent payouts.

Although the fund has seen its share price rise, NIE still trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. This means that it's still a solid time to accumulate shares for long-term income investors. The fund provides instant diversity across some of the highest quality businesses in the world, but investors should limit their expectations around its ability to provide attractive price growth over time. The dividends are likely to remain consistent for long-term investors, but there are some weaknesses that investors should consider. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy NIE implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, NIE has total net managed assets of $768.5M that is spread across 203 different holdings. Under normal market conditions, the fund will invest at least 80% of its assets into a combination of equities and convertible securities. These convertible securities operate as a hybrid instrument that generates income similar to a bond, but can be converted to common shares if management believes that a position has the potential for attractive growth in the future. The breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 56.92%
  • Convertible Securities: 37.57%
  • Cash: 5.51%

Although NIE doesn't specifically aim to mimic any specific industry, the fund's breakdown is a bit similar to the S&P 500. For instance, the fund is most heavily allocated to the Information Technology companies at 34.89% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which account for 11.36% and 11.27% of assets, respectively. When I previously covered NIE, the exposure to Information Technology only made up 25.82% of assets, so the fund has increased its allocation here quite a bit.

Since the fund is actively managed, there is a listed net expense ratio of 1.07%, which may be worth it considering the high yield and complexity of the fund's approach. Additionally, NIE implements some option writing against a portion of its assets to help amplify the income level that is generated within its holdings. However, the fund limits the option writing to 70% of the value of each stock position, but this can shift based on the market environment. This allows the fund to collect option premiums, which are then used to support the higher distributions.

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to technology companies, we can see that NIE provides exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. As we can see below, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest individual holding at 5.34% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other notable names such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Alphabet (GOOG), just to name a few.

Performance Insights & Weakness

Despite the fund providing exposure to some of the most rapidly growing companies in the world, the inclusion of an option strategy and the convertible structure limit the growth potential of the fund. To demonstrate this weakness, we can refer to the performance chart over the last ten years below. As we can see, NIE's share price has increased by about 36.9% over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 250% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

So I want to emphasize that a position in NIE only makes sense if you are an investor that is specifically seeking income generation. The fund's structure and prioritization of income have resulted in lower returns over time. If you are bullish on the outlook of the market and technology, you'd likely be better off with a more traditional index-tracking fund that can fully participate in the growth of the market. By choosing income generation today, you are actively choosing to have less money at the end of the road.

There is a lot of potential growth in the market over the next decade, and NIE may not be the best choice for investors looking to participate. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Despite the fund providing exposure to companies that can directly capitalize on this growth, NIE's structure will ultimately limit returns.

This is why NIE consistently underperforms against more traditional index-tracking ETFs, such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). As we can see below, NIE's share price growth is minimal when compared to QQQ's growth of 444.7% and SPY's growth of 227.9%. So even though NIE provides a high dividend yield, this doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time. However, this doesn't necessarily make NIE a bad fund. This just confirms that NIE is best utilized by income investors that may not be prioritizing total return potential.

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, NIE traded at a discount to NAV of 8.4%. Due to the recent market pullback, NIE now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 10.07%. For reference, NIE has traded at an average discount to NAV of 10.03% over the last five years, so the slightly larger discount pricing can be an indication that it's a good time to accumulate shares. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that NIE's current price to NAV level is attractive relative to its historical range.

Since the time of my last coverage, NIE has released an updated semi-annual report for 2026. Net investment income has amounted to $0.08 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.22 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the year, NIE is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with its prior years. Unfortunately, net investment income levels are extremely weak, so this leaves the fund reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

Thankfully, technology stocks have continued to rise, and this has allowed NIE to capture net realized gains that can support the distributions paid. Additionally, the semi-annual report is only dated through the end of July, so the sector's growth in the following months isn't reflected here. The current portfolio structure and sector allocation has allowed NIE to provide an attractive level of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $30.91 per share
  • 2022: $30.32 per share
  • 2023: $22.43 per share
  • 2024: $23.82 per share
  • 2025: $26.52 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $26.82 per share

Solid Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.50 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.8%. Looking at the payout history below, NIE has done an excellent job at maintaining consistent payouts over the last decade. The two spikes you can see in 2022 and 2023 were due to special distributions that paid out excess earnings. Since NIE has proven its ability to increase its NAV over time and produce earnings that exceed payouts, I believe that this makes NIE a great option for investors seeking a source of high-income generation.

The only drawback is that the distributions aren't paid out on a monthly basis. This may not be a big deal for some investors, but retirees that are seeking a supplemental source of income to fund lifestyle expenses may prefer these distributions hitting their account more frequently. If we take the fund's quarterly distribution and multiply it by four quarters, we get an estimated annual payout of $2.00 per share. The fund's latest reporting indicates that NIE has no problem generating earnings that can exceed this payout amount, which means that a dividend cut in the near-term is very unlikely.

Additionally, the fund aims to issue payouts in a tax-efficient manner if possible. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 71.2% of the distributions on a YTD basis were paid out using net realized long-term capital gains. Long-term capital gains are classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. So even though the remaining portion of the distributions YTD were fueled by net realized short-term gains and net investment income, the majority focus on tax-efficient payouts limits the overall tax liability for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating at this time due to the attractive valuation and the potential to provide consistent income over time. The fund's strategy has proven to be efficient at participating in the growth of the market. Although the included option strategy and emphasis on income may limit long-term returns when compared to more traditional index-tracking ETFs, NIE can still be a great choice for investors that specifically want to prioritize a reliable stream of income while still getting equity exposure to some of the best growth companies in the world.

data.title: Waiting For A Sudden Surge: The Eurozone's Big 4
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski | Charlotte de Montpellier | Paolo Pizzoli | Ruben Dewitte

Germany: Waiting for the ketchup bottle effect

Germany has been both the eurozone’s greatest disappointment and its main source of hope this year. Mired in prolonged stagnation and burdened by many structural challenges, Germany remained the region’s growth laggard. At the same time, its historic U-turn on fiscal stimulus and the announcement of major infrastructure and defence investments raised expectations for a rebound, both for Germany and the eurozone as a whole. However, persistent political tensions and the absence of meaningful structural reforms have dampened these hopes. Even the planned public investments for 2025 have fallen significantly short of target. The sauce is stuck in the bottle.

So, the main question is whether Germany is fundamentally unable to make fiscal stimulus effective – or whether it is simply a matter of time. We believe it is the latter. Fiscal stimulus and investment often resemble a ketchup bottle: nothing comes out at first, and then suddenly everything pours out at once. While this won’t solve Germany’s structural problems, it should deliver a boost. We expect German GDP growth to approach 1% in 2026 and 2% in 2027.

France: Persistent fiscal deadlock and rising risk of early elections

A new budget is unlikely before year-end, as political manoeuvring ahead of the 2027 presidential race is blocking consensus between the Senate and National Assembly. The most probable outcome is a rollover of the 2025 budget, with debates restarting almost from scratch in January. An agreement will remain elusive, raising the risk of government collapse and early legislative elections next year.

Although the right-wing RN leads polls at around 35%, it is unlikely to win a majority, meaning new elections would do little to resolve the deadlock. Without a budget, fiscal efforts will falter, and public finances will deteriorate, with the deficit widening to about 5.7% of GDP if the situation persists throughout 2026, up from 5.4% in 2025, well above the 4.6% target promised by the Lecornu government. While the absence of fiscal tightening may offer some support, higher financing costs and persistent uncertainty will offset this, leaving growth at just 0.8% versus 1.1% for the eurozone.

Italy: Still a domestic demand story

The effect of higher US tariffs on Italian external accounts will likely play out more clearly over 2026, when we expect net exports to act as a drag on economic growth. This will shift the burden of growth to domestic demand. Gross fixed capital formation should remain the primary driver, supported by infrastructure investment foreseen under the EU-funded National Recovery and Resilience Plan, which is due to formally expire over the summer of 2026.

Non-restrictive European Central Bank monetary policy could also help revive machinery investment. Private consumption, which has been disappointingly weak over 2025, might catch up a little next year. A resilient labour market, the ongoing recovery in purchasing power driven by decent wage dynamics and low inflation, and an extension of personal income tax reductions introduced by the budget might encourage households to reduce their savings ratios from current high levels.

Fiscal policy, however, will provide little additional support. The draft budget under discussion in parliament is neutral by design, reflecting the government’s clear priority of maintaining the adjustment path and ensuring Italy exits the Excessive Deficit Procedure in early 2026.

Spain: Looking for a quality turning point

Spain’s economy remains a eurozone outperformer, with 2025 growth nearing 3%. However, much of this momentum is quantity-driven: immigration has increased the population, making Spain’s performance less impressive when measured by GDP per capita or labour productivity gains. The key challenge now is to make growth more quality-led, driven by higher productivity. This will be especially crucial in 2026, as the contribution of government spending growth will be limited in the absence of a budget, net exports will be muted partly due to the strong euro, and private consumption growth is normalising.

Investment is set to play a pivotal role, with the EU’s Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF) providing significant upside: only about €59 billion of the possible €163 billion has been awarded so far. While the productivity impact may not be immediate, these investments could mark a turning point towards quality-driven growth and help keep Spain ahead of its eurozone peers in 2026 and beyond.
